title: Women at Work: Tupperware, Passion Parties, and Beyond By L. Susan Williams and Michelle Bemiller Lynne Rienner Publishers, Inc. 2011. 273 pages. $58.50 cloth
abstract: In Women at Work: Tupperware, Passion Parties, and Beyond, L. Susan Williams and Michelle Bemiller offer a feminist analysis of what they term the “party plan economy.” This economy can be characterized by direct sales companies that sit at the border of the formal and informal economy and often rely on hegemonic gender ideologies and practices to remain profitable. Using a conversational writing style that is meant for an audience broadly interested in gender and feminist issues, Williams and Bemiller weave together a series of case studies related to the party plan economy from contributors with backgrounds in sociology, history, anthropology, law, social work and activism. Examples include a study of Tupperware, which traces the historical roots of the party plan economy to the separate spheres ideology of the 1950's, studies highlighting the patriarchal ideology and practices of Mary Kay and Pampered Chef, and a study of home-based workers in the Czech Republic.
